key_concepts: Create game assets and optimize th texturing.
[url]Blender Isometric Line Art House Tutorial | Polygon Runway^^https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1RMQSZ13YXU[/url]
Create a simple Roman-style villa with a line art outline

content: Start with a new scene and delete the camera and light. [k]Tab[/k] into Edit Mode and [k]Ctrl-R[/k] to add a horizontal Loop Cut. Move it towards the top of the cube (Image 1). With the Loop Cut selected, [k]Ctrl-B[/k] to bevel the edge. Then, [k]Alt-E,F[/k] to extrude the edges along their face normals. We will want to extrude them in (Image 2). Add another hortizontal Loop Cut towards the bottom. [k]3[/k] into Face Mode and select the top and bottom faces. [k]S,Shift-Z[/k] and scale those faces in (Image 3).

image.caption: Image 1 - Adding a Loop Cut
Image 2 - Extruding in the bevel
Image 3 - Scaling in the top and bottom

content: Select the front face on the beveled separation between top and bottom. [k]Shift-S,T[/k] to move the 3D Cursor there. Still in Edit Mode, [k]Shift-A[/k] and add a cube. Doing this in Edit Mode will make it part of the same mesh. Scale it down until it is the right shape and size for a locking mechanism (Image 4). Add a Loop Cut across the cube so that we can adjust the front face without changing the back too much (Image 5). Select the front face and [k]S[/k] to scale it in, [k]I[/k] to add an Inset, and then [k]E[/k] to extrude that inwards (Image 6). If you wanted to scale up or down the lock, selecting one of the lock faces and [k]L[/k] would select only faces attached to the lock.

image.caption: Image 4 - Adding a Cube for the lock
Image 5 - Adding a Loop Cut
Image 6 - Extruding in the lock

content: Still in Edit Mode, check the faces to see if there is any z-fighting, and also make sure all faces are oriented correctly. [k]A[/k] to select all topology, and then [k]U,S[/k] to bring up the Smart UV Project dialog. Set the Island Margin to 0.02 and click OK. Go into UV Editing Workspace to see how Blender did (Image 7).

image.caption: Image 7 - Reviewing the unwrapping

content: Go to the Texture Paint Workspace. In the UV side of the screen, click New to create a new Image Texture (Image 8). Give the new image a name, and set its size to 2048x2048 to make it a 2K image (Image 9). Now we want to apply that image. In the Texture Paint side of the screen, click the Texture Slots dropdown and click the button to browse for existing images (Image 10). Apply the image that was just created.

image.caption: Image 8 - Creating a new Image Texture
Image 9 - Setting the texture size
Image 10 - Assigning the texture to the mesh

content: Let's paint the bottom of the mesh blue. Choose a deep blue color for the mesh; you can do this from the color swatch at the top or from the Brush Setting menu on the right. Click the Fill Button from the menu on the right, or press [k]Shift-Space,5[/k]. Click the mesh to fill it with the blue (Image 11). You'll see this reflected not only on the mesh but in the UV Texture Image. Now let's make the top red. [k]Tab[/k] into Edit Mode, [k]3[/k] for Face Selection, and select the top face. [k]Ctrl-Numpad+[/k] to select the adjoining faces (do it twice). [k]Tab[/k] back into Texture Paint mode, and click the button for Paint Mask (Image 12). This will make only the selected faces active for painting. Switch to a deep red color and fill the top faces (Image 13).

image.caption: Image 11 - Filling the texture with blue
Image 12 - The Paint Mask button
Image 13 - Filling the top with a base red color

content: We want to draw some scuff marks on the crates, especially around the edges. Click the Draw button in the Texture Paint view, or [k]Shift-Spacebar,1[/k]. Open the colorpicker and click the eyedropper or [k]Shift-X[/k] and sample the color from the top or bottom. We want use this color, but a less saturated version of it to add scuff marks at the edges. [k]Tab[/k] into Edit Mode, and select one face to start with and then [k]Tab[/k] back into TexturePaint Mode. [k]F[/k] will allow you to enlarge/shrink the radius of the drawing brush, and [k]Shift-F[/k] will let you modify the strength. Play around with different results. If you start drawing on the face and nothing happens, your model may have been accidentally assigned a different texture. Go into the Texture section of the Tool Menu, and clear out any texture that was set (Image 14).

image.caption: Image 14 - Clear any textures that were set
Image 12 - The Paint Mask button
Image 13 - Filling the top with a base red color

key_concepts: Model and animate a sci fi-  themed vehicle for export to game engines.

content: Open a new scene in Blender [k]Ctrl-N,G[/k]. [k]Delete[/k] the Camera, and Light. In Edit Mode, select the Cube and [k]G,Z,1[/k] to move it 1m up, above the origin (Image 1). Change the Transform Pivot Point to 3D Cursor (Image 2) and [k]S,4[/k] to scale the cube up (Image 3). Change the Transform Pivot Point back to Median Point (Image 4). [k]Tab[/k] into Edit Mode and select the top face. [k]G,Z,-1[/k] to move it down a little (Image 5).

image.caption: Image 1 - Opening a new scene, deleting the Camera and Light, going into Edit Mode and moving the Cube 1m up above the origin
Image 2 - Changing the Transform Pivot Point to 3D Cursor
Image 3 - Scaling the cube up
Image 4 - Changing the Transform Pivot Point back to Median Point
Image 5 - Going into Edit Mode, selecting the top face and moving it down a little

content: In Edge Mode, select the front and back edges and [k]RMB,S[/k] to subdivide them (Image 6). Select the new edge and [k]G,Z[/k] to form the roof (Image 7). [k]1[/k] into Vertex Mode and select the opposite vertices in the front and back faces and [k]J[/k] to join them (Image 8).

image.caption: Image 6 - Going into Edge Mode, selecting the front and back edges and subdividing them
Image 7 - Selecting the new edge and moving it in the z-axis to form the roof
Image 8 - Going into Vertex Mode, selecting the opposite vertices in the front and back faces and joining them

content: Go into Face Mode and delete the bottom face (Image 9). Move around the the top and select the faces of the roof. [k]P,S[/k] to separate those pieces and rename them in the Scene Collection (Image 10). Repeat this for the gables (Image 11).

image.caption: Image 9 - Going into Face Mode and deleting the bottom face
Image 10 - Moving to the top, selecting the faces of the roof, separating those pieces and renaming them in the Scene Collection
Image 11 - Repeating this for the gables

content: [k]Ctrl-R,Enter,Enter[/k] to add a vertical Loop Cut under the gable (Image 12). [k]Ctrl-B[/k] to add a bevel for the door (Image 13). Add two more Loop Cuts, one towards the roof, another in the middle (Image 14). Rotate to the side and add a vertical Loop Cut down the middle (Image 15). Bevel this edge as well; this will be the window (Image 16).

image.caption: Image 12 - Adding a vertical Loop Cut under the gable
Image 13 - Adding a bevel for the door
Image 14 - Adding two more Loop Cuts, one towards the roof and another in the middle
Image 15 - Rotating to the side and adding a vertical Loop Cut down the middle
Image 16 - Beveling this edge as well for the window

content: Select the door and window faces and [k]Alt-E,F[/k] to extrude the faces along their normals and move them in (Image 17). Select the face at the bottom of the door frame and [k]X,F[/k] to delete it (Image 18).

image.caption: Image 17 - Selecting the door and window faces and extruding them along their normals and moving them in
Image 18 - Selecting the face at the bottom of the door frame and deleting it

content: Go to Object Mode to select the gable mesh, and in Edit Mode, delete one of the faces. Add a Mirror Modifier in the y-axis to add the opposite side to the scene. Repeat for the roof mesh, mirroring in the x-axis (Image 19). Make sure to enable clipping for both modifiers. Still in Edit Mode with the roof, add a Loop Cut and Bevel it to create the ridges on the side of the roof (Image 20). [k]Alt,LMB[/k] the bottom edge to select all edges and [k]G,G[/k] and hold down [k]Alt[/k] to extrude the roof down to create an overhang (Image 21). Select the middle face of the roof and [k]P,S[/k] to separate it into a new mesh (Image 22).

image.caption: Image 19 - Going to Object Mode, selecting the gable mesh, deleting one face in Edit Mode, adding a Mirror Modifier in the y-axis, and repeating for the roof mesh mirroring in the x-axis
Image 20 - Adding a Loop Cut and beveling it to create the ridges on the side of the roof
Image 21 - Selecting all bottom edges and extruding the roof down to create an overhang
Image 22 - Selecting the middle face of the roof and separating it into a new mesh

content: Select the two outside edges of the roof and [k]S,Y[/k] to scale them out (Image 23). [k]A[/k] to select all faces and [k]Alt-E,F[/k] to extrude the out along normals to raise them outwards (Image 24). Select the underneath face and [k]Alt-S[/k] to bring the bottom side down (Image 25). If you want to even it out, select the bottom edge and then top edge [k]G,G[/k] to Edge Slide them towards the opposite edge (Image 26). Delete the vertices on the opposite side and update Mirror Modifier to include the y-axis.

image.caption: Image 19 - Scaling the roof out along the y-axis
Image 24 - Raising the ridges
Image 21 - Bringing down the opposite face
Image 26 - Sliding the edge to make the beam more even

content: Back in Object Mode select the roof and go into Edit Mode. Select the bottom edge and [k]G,G[/k] to Edge Slide it up to give some space for the beams on the side (Image 27).

image.caption: Image 27 - Sliding the roof up

content: Go into Object Mode and select the house mesh. [k]Tab[/k], [k]1[/k] to go into Edit Mode, Vertex Selection Mode. Select a vertex on one of the bottom corners, and [k]Shift-S,T[/k] to move the 3D Cursor there (Image 28). [k]Tab[/k] into Object Mode and [k]Shift-A > Mesh > Plane[/k] to add a Plane at that spot (Image 29). [k]S[/k] to scale it down (Image 30). [k]G,Shift-Z[/k] to move it in somewhat so it's not too thick; use the dialog to make sure the movement is uniform (Image 31).

image.caption: Image 28 - Positioning the 3D Cursor at the corner of the house
Image 28 - Adding a Plane
Image 28 - Scaling the Plane
Image 31 - Moving the Plane towards the center of the house

content: In Edit Mode [k]E[/k] to extrude the beam up (Image 32). [k]Numpad1[/k] into Front View and [k]Alt-Z[/k] for X-Ray Mode. Select the top vertices and [k]Shift-Ctrl-Alt-S,Y[/k] to skew the top (Image 33) and then [k]G,Z[/k] to move it down (Image 34). [k]Numpad3[/k] into Side View and [k]G,Y[/k] to line it up with the roof beams from the side (Image 35). Finally, add a Mirror Modifier using the house mesh as the Mirror Object, in x- and y-axes (Image 36).

image.caption: Image 32 - Extruding the beam up.
Image 33 - Skewing the top of the beam
Image 34 - Moving the beam down below the roof beams
Image 35 - Lining up the beams from the side
Image 36 - Adding a Mirror Modifier

content: Select the house mesh again, and this time select the edge at the top above the door, and [k]Shift-S,T[/k] to set the 3D Cursor there (Image 37). Again add a Plane, scale it down and [k]R,X,90[/k] to rotate it (Image 38). Scale it along the x-axis and then extrude it out in the y-axis(Image 39). Finish it off with a Mirror Modifier in the y-axis (Image 40).

image.caption: Image 37 - Positioning the 3D Cursor above the door
Image 28 - Adding a Plane for the beam and scaling and rotating
Image 39 - Scaling the Plane in the x-axis and extruding in the y-axis
Image 40 - Adding a Mirror Modifier in the y-axis

content: Select the house mesh and go into Edit Mode. [k]3[/k] into Face Mode and [k]Alt-LMB[/k] to select the bottom face loop (Image 41). [k]Shift-D,LMB[/k] to duplicate in plance and [k]P,S[/k] to separate the selection (Image 42). [k]Tab[/k] back into Object Mode and select the foundation mesh. Go into Edit Mode, Face Mode, and select the three faces by the door and [k]X,F[/k] to delete them (Image 43). [k]2[/k] into Edge Mode, select the top edge loop and [k]G,Z[/k] to move it down (Image 44).

image.caption: Image 41 - Selecting the bottom Face Loop
Image 42 - Duplicating and separating the bottom Face Loop
Image 43 - Deleting the faces by the door
Image 44 - Lowering the top edge

content: Rotate around to the back and [k]Ctrl-R[/k] to add some Loop Cuts (Image 45). Do the same in the front (Image 46). [k]A[/k] to select all vertices and [k]Alt-E,F,S[/k] to extrude the faces out along normals (the [k]S[/k] does it at even scaling) (Image 47). Select the top edge loop and [k]G,Z[/k] to bring it down (Image 48).

image.caption: Image 45 - Adding topology to the rear using Loop Cuts
Image 46 - Adding topology to the front using Loop Cuts
Image 47 - Extruding the faces out at even scaling
Image 48 - Bringing the top Edge Loop down

content: Go around the mesh and [k]G,Z[/k] to move some edges up to create some variation (Image 49). Rotate around to the bottom and delete the bottom faces that won't be seen (Image 50).

image.caption: Image 49 - Adding variation
Image 50 - Deleting the bottom faces.
Image 47 - Extruding the faces out at even scaling
Image 48 - Bringing the top Edge Loop down

content: Select the house mesh and go into Edit Mode. [k]3[/k] into Face Mode and [k]Alt-LMB[/k] to select the faces for the door frame, [k]Shift-D,LMB[/k] to duplicate in place and [k]P,S[/k] to separate the selection (Image 51). [k]Tab[/k] back into Object Mode and select the door frame mesh. Go into Edit Mode,select all faces and [k]Alt-E,F,S[/k] to extrude them inwards by scale (Image 52). Select the faces in front and [k]E,Y[/k] to extrude them out (Image 53). In Edge Mode, select the inner edges at the front and [k]Shift-D,LMB[/k] and [k]P,S[/k] to separate just the edges (Image 54). Go into Edit Mode with that mesh, [k]A[/k] to select all edges and [k]F[/k] to fill it to create the door. Finish by [k]G,Y[/k] moving it back (Image 55).

image.caption: Image 51 - Duplicating a separating faces for the door frame
Image 52 - Extruding the door frame inwards
Image 53 - Extruding the door frame out along the y-axis
Image 54 - Separating the inner edge to make the door
Image 55 - Filling the door and moving it in

content: Rotate around to the back and [k]Ctrl-R[/k] to add some Loop Cuts (Image 45). Do the same in the front (Image 46). [k]A[/k] to select all vertices and [k]Alt-E,F,S[/k] to extrude the faces out along normals (the [k]S[/k] does it at even scaling) (Image 47). Select the top edge loop and [k]G,Z[/k] to bring it down (Image 48).

image.caption: Image 45 - Adding topology to the rear using Loop Cuts
Image 46 - Adding topology to the front using Loop Cuts
Image 47 - Extruding the faces out at even scaling
Image 48 - Bringing the top Edge Loop down

content: Go around the mesh and [k]G,Z[/k] to move some edges up to create some variation (Image 49). Rotate around to the bottom and delete the bottom faces that won't be seen (Image 50).

image.caption: Image 49 - Adding variation
Image 50 - Deleting the bottom faces.
Image 47 - Extruding the faces out at even scaling
Image 48 - Bringing the top Edge Loop down

key_concepts: [url]Low Poly Treasure Chest in Blender 3.0 Full Tutorial | Polygon Runway^^https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HJf9G4NMdt4[/url]
Model a low poly treasure chest.
Model and animate a low poly treasure chest.
Windows 98 PC in PS1-style low resolution graphics.
